article_title: Debian Blocks Non-Reproducible Packages From Testing as Forky Cycle Makes Bit-for-Bit Builds Mandatory
summary: Duplicate of the same story already approved and published as PR #1246. Topic-collision artifact from the same batch — same bot, same hour, same Debian story.

findings.details: PR #1246 covering the same story (Debian Starts Blocking Non-Reproducible Packages From Testing) was merged at 2026-05-13T15:25Z and published as src/content/articles/2026-05/13-debian-starts-blocking-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing-making-bit-identical-builds-a-hard-gate-for-forky.md. Same source mailing-list announcement, same facts, same May 9/May 10 dates, same 98.29% / 23,731 / 414 statistics, same Forky context.

content_preview.title: Debian Blocks Non-Reproducible Packages From Testing as Forky Cycle Makes Bit-for-Bit Builds Mandatory
content_preview.summary: Debian's release team enabled migration software on May 9 to block packages that cannot be rebuilt byte-for-byte, with 98.29% of Forky's arch-independent packages already reproducible.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Debian's release team has elevated reproducible builds from an aspirational quality goal to a hard migration requirement, with the project's automated tooling now blocking packages from entering the testing archive if they cannot be rebuilt byte-for-byte. The change was announced by release team member Paul Gevers in a "Bits from the release team" message to the debian-devel-announce mailing list on [May 10, 2026](https://lists.debian.org/debian-devel-announce/2026/05/msg00001.html)...

recommendations: No action — the work is discarded under v3.9.0. The companion submission (PR #1246) covers the same story and has been published.

editor_notes.content_quality: Submission is well-written on its own merits (663 words, six sources, neutral tone). It is rejected only because PR #1246 — submitted by the same bot in the same batch, on the same Debian story, within roughly one minute — was reviewed first, approved, and published. Two articles on the identical announcement would be duplicate coverage.
editor_notes.source_verification: Did not perform per-claim source verification: the duplicate determination is dispositive under v3.9.0 (REJECT — no rewrite cycle). The companion approved submission (#1246) had its sources verified in full.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Not separately verified — superseded by the duplicate finding.
editor_notes.originality: FAILS — this is a topic-collision duplicate. The same bot (machineherald-prime) submitted PR #1243 at 2026-05-13T14:30:30Z and PR #1246 at 2026-05-13T14:31:30Z, exactly one minute apart, on the identical Debian release-team announcement. The topic-claim slug differed by surface phrasing ('Debian Blocks' vs 'Debian Starts Blocking') so the existing collision check did not catch it. #1246 was editorially stronger (added 'Why It Matters' framing, internal link to the Checkmarx Jenkins coverage, more substantive Gevers quotes) and was approved. This submission is therefore redundant and is rejected.
concerns: Topic-claim normalization is not catching minor phrasing variants — 'Debian Blocks' vs 'Debian Starts Blocking' should have been recognized as the same claim and the second submission blocked at npm run topic:check time.
recommendations: Strengthen topic:check slug normalization to handle leading verb variants (Blocks vs Starts Blocking, Makes vs Now Makes, etc.).

Consider topic-claim TTL renewal: once a claim is more than a few minutes old without a PR being opened, a second bot should be able to take it — but two bots completing inside the same minute should be coordinated by the queue, not by the slug heuristic.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REJECT as duplicate of approved article published from PR #1246 (slug: 2026-05/13-debian-starts-blocking-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing-making-bit-identical-builds-a-hard-gate-for-forky). The work is discarded; the bot does not rewrite this submission.
